DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-27-2012 Sustained
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Elisa T. Tolentino, Esq., Deputy City Attorney, Tel. 408.535.1900, appearing for defendant and moving party.
(Plaintiff David M Folmar appeared late at the hearing.)

No other appearance when the matter was called. The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT CITY OF SAN JOSE'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT is Sustained without leave to amend. The Court notes that venue does not appear to lie in this Court, but it also appears that defendant has waived this basis, at least at this time. The complaint is incoherent, and thus the demurrer for uncertainty is sustained. While such a ruling normally includes leave to amend, at least on an initial demurrer, leave to amend will not be granted because there are other, and fatal, bases requiring the court to sustain the demurrer. To the extent that Plaintiff attempts to state causes of action for intentional tort, attempted intentional tort, conspiracy, insurance fraud, and stalking, there is no statutory basis for these causes of action and Defendant is therefore immune from liability. Govt. Code 815; Peterson v. San Francisco Community College Dist. (1984) 36 Cal.3d 799, 809. To the extent that Plaintiff attempts to state a cause of action for obstruction of justice, this cause of action fails because obstruction of justice is a criminal act., Pen. Code 135, and no civil right claim can be predicated upon a mere violation of a criminal statute. Agnew v. Parks (1959) 172 Cal.App.2d 756, 765. Plaintiff's claim for overtime pay that he was allegedly denied between January 13, 2003 and October 2006 (Complaint, p.8) is time-barred by the 3-year statute of limitations in CCP 338(a). Plaintiff's causes of action for harassment and retaliation under FEHA are barred because Plaintiff failed to timely exhaust his administrative remedies. The timely filing of an administrative complaint, and exhaustion of that remedy, are prerequisites to maintenance of an action for damages under the FEHA. Balloon v. Superior Court, (1995) 39 Cal.App.4th 1116, 1120, superseded on other grounds; Accardi v. Superior Court (1993) 17 Cal.App.4th 341, 349; Denney v. Universal City Studios, Inc. (1992) 10 Cal.App.4th 1226, 1232; Valdez v. City of Los Angeles (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 1043, 1052. An administrative complaint must be filed no later than one year from the date upon which the alleged unlawful practice occurred. Govt. Code 12960(d). Plaintiff resigned from his position with Defendant in November 2007. Complaint, p.3. Although Plaintiff alleges retaliatory and harassing acts that occurred after his employment ended, such conduct is not actionable. Plaintiff did not file an administrative complaint until April 2011. Complaint, p. 2. Order signed in open Court. Judge: Curtis E. A. Karnow; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Not Reported.
